title: Race Track

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: A car must complete one lap on a one-way circular race track. There are N gas cans placed at various positions around the track. The TOTAL gas across all cans is exactly enough to complete one full lap.
rules: The car starts with no gas in its tank.
Whenever the car passes a gas can, it immediately picks up that can's gas and adds it to the tank.
The car drives in one direction only.
You can choose any starting position. The car must complete a full lap from there.
setup.question: Prove that there always exists a starting position that lets the car complete the full lap.

learningObjectives: Use induction on N to reduce the problem to a smaller instance.
Identify a can whose gas is sufficient to reach the next can -- such a can must exist by an averaging argument.
Merge that pair into a single virtual can to reduce N by 1.

stages.label: Choose your technique

options.text: Induction on N: prove the base case (N=1), then show that for any N, you can MERGE two cans to reduce to N-1.
Brute force over the N possible starting positions.

stages.explanation: The natural recursion: if we can show how to reduce an N-can problem to an (N-1)-can problem with the same total-gas-equals-lap property, induction handles the rest.

stages.label: Base case
stages.prompt: For N = 1 can, can the car always complete the lap?

options.text: Yes -- the single can holds exactly 1 lap's worth of gas. Start there.
Only if the can is at a specific location.

stages.explanation: With N = 1, the can holds exactly the gas for 1 lap. Starting at the can gives a full tank, so the lap is doable.

stages.label: Inductive step: find a mergeable pair
stages.prompt: For N+1 cans with total gas = 1 lap, why must there exist some can whose gas is enough to reach the NEXT can?

options.text: If EVERY can fell short of reaching the next, the total gas (= 1 lap) would have to be less than the total track distance (= 1 lap) -- contradiction. So at least one can has enough gas to reach its successor.
By the largest-can argument: the can with the most gas is enough.

stages.explanation: Averaging argument: total gas = total distance = 1 lap. If can_i had less than segment_i for all i, summing would give total_gas < total_distance, contradicting the equality. So some can_i has gas_i >= segment_i.

stages.label: Merge the pair
stages.prompt: Suppose can i has enough gas to reach can i+1. We MERGE them: replace cans i and i+1 with a single virtual can at position i, holding gas_i + gas_{i+1}. Why is this reduction valid?

options.text: Any starting position that solves the N-can merged problem also solves the (N+1)-can original -- because reaching the merged-can position with sufficient gas in the merged version corresponds to reaching can i in the original (with enough gas to continue).
It's a simplification but not strictly equivalent.
The merged problem might have a different total gas.

stages.explanation: The merge is rigorous: if the car can complete the lap in the merged version starting at some position S, then the same starting position S works in the original (N+1)-can version, because at can i the car has enough gas to reach can i+1 directly (without 'pausing' at can i+1 specifically).

stages.label: Commit

options.text: Yes -- base case N=1 works, and any (N+1)-can instance reduces to an N-can instance via the merge. By induction, the result holds for all N.
No -- we didn't show the merging preserves the total gas.
No -- the merged can might not be at an optimal position.

stages.explanation: The merge preserves total gas (gas_i + gas_{i+1} stays the same) and total distance (it's still one lap). So the inductive hypothesis applies to the smaller instance, and the original starting position pulls back from there.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: There's a non-inductive proof: drive a 'phantom car' with a full tank from any starting point and record the tank level at each can. Where do you start an empty-tank car?
What if total gas exceeded one lap by epsilon? Is the conclusion still true?
Generalize: what if you have TWO laps' worth of total gas? Can you complete TWO laps from some starting position?
